stem: In the expansion of \( ([[a]]x + [[b]])^{[[n]]} \), what is the coefficient of \( x^{[[k]]} \)?
solution: The general term of \( (ax+b)^{n} \) is \( \binom{n}{k}(ax)^{k}b^{n-k} \), so the coefficient of \( x^{[[k]]} \) is \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]}([[a]])^{[[k]]}([[b]])^{[[n]]-[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is only \( \binom{n}{k} \); it omits the factors \( a^{k} \) and \( b^{n-k} \).
This swaps the exponents on \( a \) and \( b \).
This omits the binomial coefficient \( \binom{n}{k} \) entirely.
This uses the binomial coefficient for one index too low.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by two from the correct coefficient.

stem: The expansion of \( ([[a]]x + [[b]])^{[[n]]} \) has \( [[n]]+1 \) terms, since [[n]] is even. What is the coefficient of its middle term, \( x^{[[mid]]} \)?
solution: The middle term corresponds to index \( j = [[n]]/2 = [[mid]] \), where numerator and denominator exponents on \( a \) and \( b \) are equal. Its coefficient is \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[mid]]}([[a]])^{[[mid]]}([[b]])^{[[mid]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the factors \( a^{[[mid]]}b^{[[mid]]} \), leaving only the binomial coefficient.
This omits the binomial coefficient \( \binom{n}{[[mid]]} \).
This uses the binomial coefficient for one index too low.
This raises \( a \) to the full power \( n \) instead of just \( [[mid]] \).
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.

stem: What is the sum of all the coefficients in the expansion of \( ([[a]]x + [[b]])^{[[n]]} \)? (Hint: every coefficient is counted once when \( x = 1 \).)
solution: Setting \( x = 1 \) turns the expansion into the sum of all its coefficients: \( ([[a]](1) + [[b]])^{[[n]]} = ([[a]]+[[b]])^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to raise the sum to the power [[n]].
This substitutes \( x=-1 \) instead of \( x=1 \).
This raises \( a \) and \( b \) to the power separately instead of raising their sum.
This multiplies the sum by [[n]] instead of raising it to the power [[n]].
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

stem: In the expansion of \( ([[a]]x + [[b]])^{[[n]]} \), what is the sum of the coefficients of only the terms with an EVEN power of \( x \) (counting a constant term as the power \( 0 \))?
solution: Let \( f(x) = ([[a]]x+[[b]])^{[[n]]} \). Then \( f(1) \) sums ALL coefficients and \( f(-1) \) sums coefficients with alternating sign, so the even-power sum is \( \dfrac{f(1)+f(-1)}{2} = \dfrac{([[a]]+[[b]])^{[[n]]} + (-[[a]]+[[b]])^{[[n]]}}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the ODD-power coefficient sum \( \frac{f(1)-f(-1)}{2} \), not the even-power sum.
This is just \( f(1) \), the sum of ALL coefficients, forgetting to combine with \( f(-1) \).
This is just \( f(-1) \) alone, not the average of \( f(1) \) and \( f(-1) \).
This forgets to divide by 2 after adding \( f(1) \) and \( f(-1) \).
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

stem: In the expansion of \( \left([[a]]x^{2} + \dfrac{[[b]]}{x}\right)^{[[n]]} \), what is the term independent of \( x \) (i.e., the constant term)?
solution: The general term is \( \binom{[[n]]}{j}(x^{2})^{[[n]]-j}\left(\dfrac{[[b]]}{x}\right)^{j} = \binom{[[n]]}{j}([[a]])^{[[n]]-j}([[b]])^{j}x^{2\cdot[[n]]-3j} \). Setting \( 2([[n]]) - 3j = 0 \) gives \( j = [[j]] \), so the constant term is \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[j]]}([[a]])^{[[n]]-[[j]]}([[b]])^{[[j]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the factors \( a^{n-j}b^{j} \), leaving only the binomial coefficient.
This swaps the exponents on \( a \) and \( b \).
This omits the binomial coefficient \( \binom{n}{j} \).
This uses the binomial coefficient for one index too low.
This is off by one from the correct term.
This is off by one from the correct term.

stem: Using the binomial theorem applied to \( (1+x)^{[[n]]}(1+x)^{[[n]]} = (1+x)^{2\cdot[[n]]} \) and comparing coefficients, evaluate \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{[[n]]} \binom{[[n]]}{k}^{2} \).
solution: By Vandermonde's identity, \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}^{2} = \binom{2n}{n} \); directly summing the [[n]]+1 squared coefficients for \( n=[[n]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the sum of the (unsquared) binomial coefficients, \( 2^{n} \), not the sum of their squares.
This squares \( n \) itself rather than summing the squares of the coefficients.
This reports a single binomial coefficient rather than the sum of all their squares.
This multiplies \( n \) by \( 2^{n} \), an unrelated quantity.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by two from the correct sum.
